description: The currently loaded image is a placeholder "Check for updates" icon, no valid visual content of the target material science figure is available for direct verification. According to the associated paper extraction results, this figure presents backscattered electron (BSE, a type of SEM) microstructure images of three Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel samples with 0 wt%, 0.01 wt%, and 0.5 wt% Zr addition respectively, all processed by vacuum arc melting, 1200 °C homogenization, hot rolling, cold rolling, and 900 °C annealing for 5 min followed by water quenching. The figure illustrates the grain structure evolution: the Zr-free sample has uniform coarse single-phase FCC grains with average size 12 μm, the 0.01 wt% Zr sample has nearly unimodal grain structure with average size 7 μm and 6% fine grains, while the 0.5 wt% Zr sample exhibits bimodal heterogeneous grain structure (65% coarse grains ~5 μm, 35% fine grains ~1 μm) with uniformly distributed 30 nm ZrC nanoprecipitates. The figure supports the conclusion that Zr addition induces heterogeneous grain structure via solute drag and Zener pinning effects, promoting strength-ductility synergy of the steel.

This set of backscattered electron (BSE, a subclass of SEM) micrographs characterizes the pre-deformation microstructures of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with different Zr addition levels. Subfigure a shows the Zr0 (0 wt% Zr) sample, which is composed of fully uniform coarse face-centered cubic (FCC) grains, with a scale bar of 25 μm. Subfigure b presents the Zr0.01 (0.01 wt% Zr) sample, dominated by coarse FCC grains with a small fraction of local fine FCC regions, with a 25 μm scale bar. Subfigure c illustrates the Zr0.50 (0.50 wt% Zr) sample, which exhibits a heterogeneous bimodal FCC structure with alternating coarse and fine grain zones, with a 50 μm scale bar. Subfigure c1 is a magnified view of the fine FCC region in Zr0.50, showing uniformly distributed fine grains with a 10 μm scale bar. The trend demonstrates that increasing Zr content induces significant grain refinement, evolving from a single coarse grain structure to a bimodal heterogeneous grain structure, which provides the microstructural basis for the improved strength-ductility synergy of the studied steel system.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of pre-deformation Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with three Zr contents: Zr0 (0 wt% Zr, subplots a-a2), Zr0.01 (0.01 wt% Zr, subplots b-b2), and Zr0.50 (0.50 wt% Zr, subplots c-c2). Each sample set includes an Inverse Pole Figure (IPF map for grain orientation characterization, a/b/c), Image Quality map (IQ map for grain morphology observation, a1/b1/c1), and Kernel Average Misorientation map (KAM map for local misorientation and dislocation density evaluation, a2/b2/c2), with a uniform scale bar of 40 μm. Zr0 steel shows equiaxed FCC grains with low KAM values corresponding to low dislocation density. Zr0.01 steel exhibits reduced average recrystallized FCC grain size with random grain orientation and slightly higher KAM values than Zr0. Zr0.50 steel presents a distinct heterogeneous grain structure consisting of coarse grain (CG) zones and fine grain (FG) zones; FG zones have much higher KAM values than CG zones, indicating high dislocation density in fine grains. The results confirm that Zr addition regulates grain structure evolution, and the heterogeneous grain structure formed at high Zr content contributes to the strength-ductility synergy of the low-density steel.

This figure presents statistical analysis of grain structures in three Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (Zr0, Zr0.01, Zr0.50, all processed by 80% cold rolling followed by 5 min annealing at 900 °C). Subplot a is a grouped bar chart showing the area fraction of coarse grain (CG) and fine grain (FG) zones: as Zr content increases from 0 wt% to 0.50 wt%, the FG fraction rises from 0% to 35%, while CG fraction reduces from 100% to 65%. Subplots b, c, d are grain size distribution histograms of FCC matrix for Zr0, Zr0.01, Zr0.50 respectively: Zr0 exhibits a unimodal distribution with average grain size ~12 μm, Zr0.01 shows a nearly unimodal distribution with average size ~7 μm with minor FG fraction, and Zr0.50 presents a bimodal distribution with peaks at ~1 μm and ~5 μm corresponding to the heterogeneous CG/FG structure. The results confirm that increasing Zr content effectively refines grains and promotes the formation of heterogeneous grain architecture, which serves as the structural basis for the improved strength-ductility synergy of the steel.

This figure includes four subplots investigating zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with three Zr content levels: Zr0 (0 wt% Zr), Zr0.01 (0.01 wt% Zr), and Zr0.50 (0.50 wt% Zr). Subplot (a) presents engineering stress-strain curves of the three steels, with an inset showing the true stress-strain curve of Zr0.50. It demonstrates that increased Zr content improves both tensile strength and fracture elongation, with Zr0.50 achieving the best strength-ductility synergy (ultimate tensile strength ~855 MPa, elongation ~58%). Subplots (b), (c), and (d) are SEM fracture morphologies of Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50 respectively, with a 10 μm scale bar. All samples show ductile fracture features with dimples, and dimple size and depth increase with rising Zr content, corresponding to improved ductility. The figure confirms that appropriate Zr addition effectively promotes strength-ductility synergy in the tested low-density steel.

This set of figures characterizes the microstructure and strain partitioning behavior of Zr0.50 zirconium-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel with heterogeneous face-centered cubic (FCC) grain structure. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD image quality (IQ) map at 0% global strain, distinguishing fine FCC grain (FG) zones and coarse FCC grain (CG) zones with a 20 μm scale bar. Subfigures (b) and (c) are von Mises strain maps obtained via quasi in-situ μ-DIC at 2% and 8% global strain respectively, both with 20 μm scale bars and a strain range of 0 to 12. At 2% global strain, high strain concentrates in CG zones and FG/CG heterointerfaces, while FG zones remain nearly undeformed. When global strain increases to 8%, local strain in FG zones becomes pronounced, confirming persistent strain partitioning between soft CG and hard FG zones during plastic deformation. These results verify that strain partitioning induces geometrically necessary dislocations, enhancing strain hardening and enabling strength-ductility synergy of the heterogeneous steel.

This set of images presents TEM, HRTEM and selected area electron diffraction characterization of Zr0.50 steel (Fe-25Mn-7Al-0.9C-0.50Zr low-density steel) after tensile deformation. Subfigure a shows planar slip and dislocations inside FCC grains after minor deformation, with the SAED pattern calibrated as <110> FCC orientation. Subfigure b reveals dense slip bands in the heavily deformed FCC matrix, with its SAED also indexed as <110> FCC. Subfigure c displays high-density nanotwin regions, with SAED containing both <110> FCC and <110> twin diffraction spots. Subfigure c1 is an HRTEM image that clearly shows the coherent interface between nanoscale twin and FCC matrix. Subfigure d presents the interaction between mobile dislocations and ~30 nm ZrC precipitated particles, while subfigure d1 confirms these particles are ZrC with <110> orientation via FFT pattern and HRTEM lattice imaging. These results demonstrate multiple cooperative deformation mechanisms including dislocation slip, twinning-induced plasticity, and ZrC precipitation strengthening, which jointly contribute to the excellent strength-ductility synergy of the Zr-alloyed low-density steel.

This figure presents Thermo-Calc calculated thermodynamic phase fractions of Zr0.50 steel (Fe-25Mn-7Al-0.9C-0.50Zr low-density steel) as a function of temperature. The left subplot covers the temperature range of 500℃ to 1000℃: FCC phase (red curve) rises from ~0.65 at 500℃ to nearly 1.0 at 700℃ and remains stable at higher temperatures; BCC phase (blue curve) and κ carbide (purple curve) decrease from low initial values at 500℃ to 0 around 700℃. The right subplot shows low-magnitude phase fractions, where ZrC (green curve) maintains a stable value of ~0.0058 (~0.6%) above 900℃, while BCC phase fraction is almost zero. These results verify that sufficient ZrC precipitates exist at the 900℃ annealing temperature, which provides Zener pinning effect to inhibit grain boundary migration during recrystallization, supporting the formation mechanism of heterogeneous grain structure in the studied Zr-alloyed low-density steel.
